To derive the total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ we first calculate the response time for each database individually based on the number of queries it handles. 

MySQL handles $ \alpha Q $ queries so its response time is $ T_{\text{MySQL}}(\alpha Q) = a \cdot (\alpha Q)^2 + b \cdot e^{c(\alpha Q)} $.

Oracle handles $ \beta Q $ queries so its response time is $ T_{\text{Oracle}}(\beta Q) = d \cdot \ln(\beta Q + 1) + \frac{e}{\beta Q + 1} $.

Sybase handles $ (1 - \alpha - \beta)Q $ queries so its response time is $ T_{\text{Sybase}}((1 - \alpha - \beta)Q) = f \cdot ((1 - \alpha - \beta)Q)^{\frac{3}{2}} - g \cdot \sqrt{(1 - \alpha - \beta)Q} $.

The total query response time function is the sum of the response times from each database. 

The total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ is the sum of the individual response times for each database. 

The total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ is derived by summing the response time functions for MySQL Oracle and Sybase. 

The total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ is expressed as $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) = T_{\text{MySQL}}(\alpha Q) + T_{\text{Oracle}}(\beta Q) + T_{\text{Sybase}}((1 - \alpha - \beta)Q) $. 

The total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ is defined in terms of the number of queries each database handles. 

The total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ is calculated using the given response time functions for each database. 

The total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ is a function of the proportions $ \alpha $ and $ \beta $ and the total number of queries $ Q $.
